w:r.w:t: Jason Company, a Philippine Corporation, sold inventory on credit to a Japanese Company on April 8, 2020. Jason received payment of 35,000 yens on May 8, 2020. The exchange rate was P1 = Y0.65 on April 8 and P1 = Y0.70 on May 8. What amount of foreign exchange gain or loss should be recognized? (rounded to the nearest peso)
Jane had the following foreign currencies transactions during 2020:
Purchased merchandise from a foreign supplier on January 20, 2020, for the Philippine peso equivalent to P60,000 and paid the invoice on April 20, 2020, at the Philippine peso equivalent to P68,000.

On September 1, 2020, borrowed the Philippine peso equivalent of P300,000 evidenced by a note that is payable in the lender’s local currency on September 1, 2021. On December 31, 2020, the Philippine peso equivalent of the principal amount of P320,000.
In Jane’s 2020 income statement, what amount should be included is a foreign exchange loss?
Assuming that Joshua entered into no forward contract, how much foreign exchange gain or loss it reports on its 2020 income statement about this transaction?

Assuming that Joshua entered into a forward contract to sell 10 million foreign currencies on December 1, 2020, as a fair value of hedge of foreign currency receivable, what is the net impact on its net income in 2020 resulting from a fluctuation in the value of the foreign currencies?

PNO Corporation subsidiary buys marketable equity securities and inventory on April 1, 2020, for 100,000 foreign currencies each. It pays for both items on June 1, 2020, and they are still on hand at year-end. Inventory is carried at a cost under the lower-of-cost-or market value. Currency exchange rates for 1 peso follow:
Assume that the foreign currency is the subsidiary’s functional currency. What balances does a consolidated balance sheet report as of December 31, 2020?

Aly Corporation subsidiary buys marketable equity securities and inventory on April 1, 2020, for 100,000 foreign currencies each. It pays for both items on June 1, 2020, and they are still on hand at year-end. Inventory is carried at a cost under the lower-of-cost-or market value. Currency exchange rates for 1 peso follow:
Assume that the peso is the subsidiary’s functional currency. What balances does a consolidated balance sheet report as of December 31, 2020?

On January 1, 2020, Leonardo Co. sold equipment to its subsidiary, Bernie Corp., for P115,000. The equipment had cost P 125,000, and the balance in accumulated depreciation was P45,000. The equipment has estimated remaining useful life of eight years and no salvage value. Both companies use straight-line depreciation. On their separate 2014 income statements, Leonardo and Bernie reported depreciation expense of P84,000 and P60,000, respectively. The amount of depreciation expense on the consolidated income statement for 2020 would have been
What is the remeasurement gain to be recognized on the consolidated income statement?
Which of the following items is not remeasurement using historical exchange rate under the temporal or remeasurement method?

George Corporation starts a subsidiary in a foreign country; the subsidiary's functional currency is not the currency of a hyperinflationary economy (using the current rate method). On January 1, George buys all of the subsidiary's common stock for 20,000 foreign currencies (FC). On April 1, the subsidiary purchases inventory for 20,000 FCs with payment made on May 1, and sells this inventory on August 1, for 30,000 FCs, which it collects on October 1. Currency exchange rates for one foreign currency are as follows:
In preparing consolidated financial statements, what translation adjustment will George report at the end of the current year?

On April 1, Quality Corporation, a Philippine Company, expects to order merchandise from a foreign supplier in three-months, denominating the transaction in foreign currencies, On April 1, the exchange rate is P 1.19 per foreign currency and Quality enters into a three-month forward contract to purchase 400,000 foreign currencies at a rate of 1.20. At the end of three months, three months, the exchange rate is P 1.21 per euro and Quality orders and receives the merchandise, paying 400,000 foreign currencies. What are the effects on net income from these transactions?
P4,000 Discount Expense plus a P4,000 negative Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received
P4,000 Discount Expense plus a P8,000 negative Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received
P4,000 Premium Expense plus a P4,000 negative Adjustment to Net income when
P8,000 Premium Expense plus a P4,000 positive Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received
P8,000 Premium Expense plus a P8,000 positive Adjustment to Net income when merchandise is received

Karen Inc., a Philippine Company, expects to order goods from a foreign supplier for 100,000 foreign currencies, with delivery and payment to be made on April 17. On January 17, Karen purchased a three-month call option on 100,000 foreign currencies and designated this. Option as a cash flow hedge of a forecasted foreign currency transaction. The following exchange rates apply:
What amount will Karen include as an option expense in net income during the period January 17 to April 17?

Mishiel Company purchases raw material from its foreign supplier, Bolivian Clay, on May 8. Payment of 2,000,000 foreign currency- units (FC) is due in 30 days. May 31 is Brisco’s fiscal year-end. The pertinent exchange rates were as follows:
How much foreign exchange gain or loss should Brisco record on May 31?

Jane, Inc., a Philippine Company, has a Japanese yen account receivable resulting from an export sale on March 1 to a customer in Japan. The exporter signed a forward contract on March 1 to sell yen and designated it’s as a cash flow hedge of a recognized receivable. The exchange rate was P0.0094 and the forward rate P0.0093. Which of the following did the Philippines exporter report in net income?
The entries on December 31, 2020, include a
Credit to Foreign Currency Payable to Exchange Broker, P4,0000
Debit to Foreign Currency Receivable from Exchange Broker, P6,000
Debit to Foreign Currency Receivable from Exchange Broker, P186,000
Debit Foreign Currency Transaction Gain, P4,000
The entries on January 30, 2021, include a
Debit a Pesos Payable to Exchange Broker, P180,000
Credit to Premium on Forward Contract, P4,000
Credit to Foreign Currency Receivable from Exchange Broker, P180,000
The entries on January 30, 2021, include a
Credit a Foreign Currency Units (Bahts), P 184,000
Debit to Foreign Currency transaction Loss, P4,000
Debit to Pesos Payable to Exchange Broker, P184,000
The entries on January 30, 2020, include a
Debit to Pesos Payable to Exchange Broker, P184,000
Credit to foreign Currency Transaction Gain, P4,000
Credit to Foreign Currency Receivable from Exchange Broker, P180,000
Debit to Foreign Currency Units (Bahts), P 184,000
The present value of the average excess earnings over the next four years discounted at 15%. (The present value of an ordinary annuity of I at I 5% for four periods is 2.85498)
The capitalization of the average excess earnings of Sayo Na Co. at 18%.
The purchase of average excess earnings over the next four years.
The capitalization of average excess earnings of Sayo Na Co. at 24%.
If Jun were to pay P23,100,000 to purchase the assets and assume the liabilities of Sayo Na Co., how much would be charged to goodwill?
Goodwill is equal to the capitalization of excess earnings at 15%.
Goodwill is equal to 3 years of excess earnings.
Goodwill is equal to the present value of excess earnings discounted at 15% for three years. (The present value factor of an ordinary annuity of 1 at 15% for three periods is 2.28323.)
A company is considering purchasing another company, which has the following assets and liabilities.
If the purchase price is 12,600,000, the amount of goodwill to be charged in recording the acquisition is

On April 1, 2020, AV Corp. acquired 80% of the outstanding stocks of SR Corp. for P2,500,000. SR Corp.'s stockholders' equity at the end of 2020 is as follows: Common stock, P80 par P2, 000,000, additional paid-in capital P500,000, and Retained Earnings P750,000. The fair value of the non- controlling interest is P685,000. All the assets of SR were fairly valued except for its inventories, which are undervalued by P90,000, Land, which is undervalued by P50,000, and the Patent, which is undervalued by P125,000. The said patent has a remaining useful life of five years. Both companies use the straight-line method for depreciation and amortization. Shareholders' equity of AV Corp. on December 31, 2020, is composed of Common stock, P50 par P3,500,000, APIC P750,000, and retained earnings P2,460,000. Goodwill, if any, should be decreased by P22,500 every year-end. No additional issuance of capital stocks occurred.

The Antony Company acquired a 60% interest in the Jeano Company when Jeano's equity comprised share capital of P100,000 and retained earnings of P150,000. Jeano's current statement of financial position shows share capital of P100,000, a revaluation reserve of P75,000 and retained earnings of P300,000.
Under PAS27 Consolidated and separate financial statements, what figure in respect of the noncontrolling interest should be included in Antony's consolidated statement of financial position?

On October 1, 2020, The Regan Company acquired 100% of the Elijah Company when the fair value of Elijah's net assets was P116 million, and its carrying amount was P120 million. The consideration transferred comprised P200 million in cash transferred at the acquisition date, plus another P60 million in cash to be transferred 11 months after the acquisition date, if a specified profit target was met by Elijah. At the acquisition date, there was only a low probability of the profit target being met, so the fair value of the additional consideration liability was P10 million. In the event, the profit target was met, and the P60 million cash was transferred.
What amount should Regan present for goodwill in its statement of consolidated financial position at December 31, 2021, according to PFRS 3 Business combinations?

100% of the equity share capital of the Richmond Company was acquired by The Janice Company on June 30, 2020. Janice issued 500,000 new P1 ordinary shares, which had a fair value of P8 of each at the acquisition date. Also, the acquisition resulted in Janice incurring fees payable to external advisers of P200,000 and share issue costs of P180,000
By PFRS 3 Business Combinations, goodwill at the acquisition gate is measured by subtracting the identifiable assets acquired and the liabilities assumed from:

Jason Co. acquired inventories on May 1, 2020, from its 70% owned subsidiary, Paz Company. The inventories were sold for P94,000, including the 25% markup on cost. Out of these inventories, 65% were sold to outsiders. During the year, Jason reported a net income of P215,000, and Paz reported a net income of P 140,000.
How much is the realized profit to be allocated to non controlling interest in 2021?

The Sylvia Company acquired a 70% interest in The Clarke Company for P1,420,000 when the fair value of Clarke's identifiable assets and liabilities was P1,200,000. Sylvia acquired a 65% interest in The Homer Company for P300,000 when the fair value of Homer's identifiable assets and liabilities was P640,000. Sylvia measures non-controlling interest at the relevant share if the identifiable net assets at the acquisition date. Neither Clarke nor Homer has any contingent liabilities at the amounts in their financial statements. Annual impairment reviews have not resulted in any impairment losses being recognized.
Under PFRS 3 Business Combinations, what is the goodwill that should be included in Sylvia's consolidated statement of financial position?

The Brown Company acquired 80% of the Ayannah Company for a consideration transferred to P100 million. The consideration was estimated to include a control premium of P24 million, Ayannah's net assets were P85 million at the acquisition date. Are the following statements true or false, according to PFRS 3 Business Combinations?
Goodwill should be measured at P32 million if the non-controlling interest is measured at its
Goodwill should be measured at P34 million if the non-controlling interest is measured at fair value?
The working paper elimination entry recorded by Parent Company on January 1, 2020, the date of acquisition of the Subsidiary Company follows:
Of the goodwill recorded, P30,000 belongs to the non-controlling interest.
What is the control premium paid by the parent company under the business combination?

Patrick Company acquired the assets (except for cash) and assumed the liabilities of Steve Company on January 2, 2020, and Steve Company is dissolved. As compensation, Patrick Company gave 24,000 shares of its common stock, 12,000 shares of its 8% preferred stock, and cash of P240,000 to the stockholders of Steve Company. On the date of acquisition, Patrick Company had the following characteristics:
Common, par value P5; fair value, P20
Preferred, par value P100; fair value, P100
Immediately before the acquisition, Steve Company's balance sheet was as follows:
An appraisal of Steve Company showed that the fair values of its assets and liabilities were equal to their book values except for the following, which had fair values as indicated:
How much must be the goodwill recognized as a result of this business combination?

On October 1, 2013, The Fernando Company acquired I 00% of The Austria Coompany when the fair value of Austria's net assets was P16 million, and their carrying amount was P120 million, The consideration transferred comprised P200 million in cash transferred at the acquisition date, plus another P60 million in cash to be transferred 11 months after the acquisition date if a specified profit target was met by Austria. At the acquisition date, there was only a low probability of the profit target being met, so the fair value of the additional consideration liability was P 10 million. In the event, the profit target was met, and the P60 million cash was transferred. What amount should Fernando present for goodwill in its statement of consolidated financial position at December 31, 2020, according to PFRS 3 Business Combinations?

PSY Corporation owns 90%, of the outstanding common shares of SVG Company. On January 2, 2020, office equipment that had a carrying value to SVG Company P480,000 and has a remaining life of 10 years was sold to PSY Corporation for P400,00. On the other hand, last August 31, 2021, PSY Corporation sole a second-hand delivery van to SVO Company at a gain of P30,000 (remaining life of 5 years).

Included in the January 1, 2021 inventory of PSY Company was merchandise inventory worth P65,000, while SVG Company had P80,000 on its December 31, 2021. These inventories came from inter-company sales and purchases. PSY Corporation included a mark-up of 25% on cost while SVG Company charged a 30% mark-up on sales. Each of the two companies has net incomes in 2020 and 2021 as follows:
What is the amount of the consolidated net income attributable to controlling interest in 2021?

Elizabeth Company acquired 100% of Louie Inc. on January 5, 2020. During 2020, Elizabeth sold Louie for P2,400,000 goods that cost P1,800,000. Louie still owned 40% of the goods at the end of the year. The cost of goods sold was P10,800,000 for Elizabeth and P6,400,000 for Louie. What was the consolidated cost of goods sold?

Michael Corporation owned 70% of the voting common stock of Gabriel Company. On January 2, 2020, Gabriel sold a parcel of land to Michael. The land had a book value of P32,000 and was sold to Michael for P45,000. Gabriel's reported net income for 2020 was 119,000. What is the non-controlling interest's share of Gabriel's net income?

Nhang Company owned all of the voting common stock of Jorge Co. On January 2, 2020, Nhana sold some equipment to Jorge for P125,000. The equipment had cost P140,000. At the time of the sale, the balance in accumulated depreciation was P40,000. The equipment had a remaining life of five years and a P-0- salvage value, Straight-line depreciation is used by both Nhang and Jorge. At what amount should the equipment (net depreciation) be included on the Consolidated balance sheet dated December 31, 2020?

The Joanne Company owns 65% of The Mary Company. On December 31, 2020, the last day of the accounting period, Joanne sold Mary a noncurrent asset for P1,000. The asset's original cost was P2,500, and on December 31, 2020, Its carrying amount in Joanne's book was P800. The group's consolidated statement of financial position has been drafted without any adjustments about this non-current asset.

Under PAS 27 Consolidated and Separate Financial Statements, what adjustments should be made to the consolidated statement of financial position figures for non-current assets and non-controlling interest?
Non-current assets (increase by P 1,500); Non-controlling Interest (increase by P525)
Non-current assets (reduced by P200); Non-controlling Interest (no change)
Non-current assets (increase by P 1,500); Non-controlling Interest (reduce by P70)
Non-current assets (increase by P 1,500); Non-controlling Interest (no change)

The Conrad Company acquired equipment on January 1, 2020, at the cost of P800,000, depreciating it over eight years with a nil residual value. On January 1, 2023, The Cris Company acquired 100% of Conrad and estimated the fair value of the equipment at P460,000, with a remaining life of 5 years. This fair value was not incorporated into Conrad's book, and depreciation expense continued to be calculated by reference to the original cost.

Under PAS 27 Consolidated and Separate Financial Statements, what adjustments should be made to the depreciation expense for the year and the statement of financial position carrying amount in preparing the consolidated financial statements for the year ended December 31, 2024?
Depreciation expense (increase by P8,Q00); Carrying Amount (increase by P24,000)
Depreciation expense (increase by ?8,000); Carrying Amount (decrease QY P24,000)
Depreciation expense (decrease by P8,0D0; Carrying Amount (increase by P24,000)
Depreciation expense (increase by P8,000); Carrying Amount (decrease by P24,000)

The Josh Company acquired an 80% interest in The Earl Company when Earl's equity comprised share capital of P100,000 and retained earnings of P500,000 . Earl's current statement of financial position shows share capital of P 100,000, a revaluation reserve of P400,000, and retained earnings o f P 1,4 00,000 .
Under PAS 27 Consolidated and separate financial statements, what figure in respect of Earl's retained earnings should be included in the consolidated statement of financial position?
The Kareen Company owns 75% of The Goldy Company. The following figures are from their separate financial statement:
Kareen Trade receivable P 1,040,000, including P 30,000 due from Goldy.
Goldy Trade receivable P 215,000, including P 40,000 due from Kareen.
Under PAS 27 Consolidated and separate financial statements, what figure should appear for trade receivables in Kareen's consolidated statement of financial position?

Cesar Company holds 90% of the common stock of Diane Company During 2020, Cesar reported sales of P1,120,000 and cost of goods sold of P 840,000. For this same period, Diane has sales of P 420,000 and cost of goods sold of P 252,000. Also, during 2020, Cesar sold merchandise to Diane for P 140,000. The subsidiary still possesses 40% of this inventory at the end of 2020.
What is the consolidated cost of goods sold?
Assuming that the transfers were from Diane Co. to Cesar Co., what is the consolidated cost of goods sold?
Assuming that the transfers were from Diane Co. to Cesar Co. what are the consolidated sales?

Baste Company owns an 80% controlling interest in the Bastion Company. Bastion regularly sells merchandise to Baste, which then sold to outside parties. The gross profit on all such sales is 40%. On .January I. 2020, Baste sold land and a building to Bastion. The value of the parcel is 20% to land and 80% to structures. The data are the following:
Cost of real estate sold on January 1, 2020
The sales price of real estate on January 1,
For 2020, what is the consolidated comprehensive income attributable to controlling interest?

Corporation Lizzy acquired 2,000 shares of the voting stock of Corporation Lizette in the open market at P48 per share. Direct costs associated with the acquisition total of P4,000. Balance sheets of both companies on January 1, 2020, immediately after the acquisition of shares of Lizzy are as follows:
The fair values of Lizzy and Lizette's assets on January 1, 2020, are presented below. Liabilities of both companies are properly valued at their respective book value:
The total consolidated assets must be
Victor Corporation owns 80 percent of the stock of Vicky Company. At the end of 2020, Victor Corporation and Vicky Company reported the following partial operating results and inventory balances:

Victor Corporation regularly prices its products at cost plus a 40 percent markup for profit. Vicky Company prices its sales at cost plus a 20 percent markup. The total sales reported by Victor and Vicky include both intercompany sales and sales to non-affiliates.
The controlling interest net income for 2020 must be

Rommel, Inc. acquired a 60% interest in Mikee Company several years ago. During 2020, Mikee sold inventory costing P75,000 to Rommel for P 100,000. A total of 16% of this inventory was not sold to an outsider until 2021. During 2021, Mikee sold inventory costing P96,000 to Rommel for P 120,000. A total of 35% of this inventory was not sold to outsiders until 2022. In 2015, Rommel reported the cost of sales of P380,000, while Mikee reported P210,000. What is the consolidated cost of sales
The following are information regarding parent and subsidiary.
Sales of P60,000 to Dean with P20,000 gross profit. Dean had P15 ,000 of this inventory on hand at year-end. Clark owns a 15% interest in Dean and does not exert significant influence.

Purchases of raw materials totaling P240,000 from Kent Corporation, a wholly-owned subsidiary. Kent’s gross profit on sale was P48,000. Clark had P60,000 of this inventory remaining on December 31, 2020
Before eliminating entries, Clark had consolidated current assets of P320,000. What amount should Clark report in its December 31, 2020, consolidated balance sheet for current assets?

Par Company owns 60% of Sub Corp's outstanding capital stock. On May 1, 2020, Par advanced Sub P70,000 in cash, which was still outstanding at December 31, 2020. What portion of this advance should be eliminated in the preparation of the December 31, 2020, consolidated balance sheet?

On 1/3/2020, PLDT sold equipment costing P1 00,000 co its 100% owned subsidiary, Idion, for P80,000. At the time of the sale, the equipment had been 50% depreciated using the straight-line method and an assigned life of I0 years. Idion continued depreciating the equipment by using the straight-line method over the remaining life of 5 years.
What is the amount of the intercompany profit or loss that must be deferred on 12/31/2020?

The Ezra Company acquired an 80% interest in the Elaine Company when Blaine's equity comprised share capital of P100,000 and retained earnings of P500,000. Elaine's current statement of financial position shows share capital of P100,000, a revaluation reserve of P400,000, and retained earnings of P1,400,000.
Under PAS 27, Consolidated and Separate Financial Statements, what figure in respect of Elaine's retained earnings should be included in the consolidated statement of financial position?

Dickie Corporation contracted to build a building for Dickson Company. The contract price was P500,000, and Dickie estimated that construction costs would total P420,000. The construction period lasted until September 1, 2020. Costs during each period, the estimated total cost of the product at the end of the year, billings and cash collected during the year were as follows:
The amount of gross profit recognized in 2021 using the percentage of completion method must be:
The following information pertains to the building contract of Carrie Construction Company, wherein the fixed contract price is P40 million.

Assume that all costs are incurred, all billings to customers are made, and all collections from costumers are received within 30 days of billing, as planned. Under the percentage-of-completion method of revenue recognition is used, how much is the income from construction for the year 2022?

Celso Construction is in its fourth year of business. Celso performs long-term construction projects and accounts for them using the percentage of completion. Celso built an apartment building at a price of P500,000. The costs and billings for this contract for the first three years are as follows:
Estimated costs yet to be incurred
Determine the income from construction in 2015.
In 2020, Jerico Construction Company was contracted to do a private road network of Philip Corporation for P50 million. The project was estimated to be completed in two years.
The construction contract provided the number of other things the following:
5% mobilization fee (to be deducted from the last billing) payable within 15 days after the signing of the contract.
Retention provision of 10% on all billings.
Progress billings on construction are payable within seven days from date of acceptance.

Jerico estimated its gross margin on the project at 25% and used the percentage of Completion method of accounting. By the end of the year, Jerico presented progress billings corresponding to 50% completion. Philip Corporation accepted all the bills presented except the last one for 10%, which was accepted on January 10. Except for the last billing of 8% accepted in 2020, which was due on January 3, 2022, all accepted billings were settled in 2020. The gross profit recognized by Jerico Construction Company for 2020 is:
The gross profit recognized by Jerico Construction Company for 2020 is:

A construction company began operations in 2020. Construction activity for the first year is shown below. All contracts are with different customers, and any work remaining on December 31, 2020, is expected to be completed in 2021.
Determine the income from construction to be reported in the income statement for the year 2020.

On July 1, 2020, DMCI Construction Company contracted to build an office building for RH Corporation for a total contract price of P9.75 million. On July 1, Mean estimated that it would take between 2 to 3 years to complete the building. On December 31, 2022, the building was deemed substantially completed. The following are accumulated contract costs incurred, total estimated costs, and accumulated billings to RH for 2020, 2021, and 2022.

Using the percentage of completion method, determine the correct income (loss) from construction to be presented in the income statement of the company for the years 2020, 2021 and 2022, respectively.

DMCI has used the cost-to-cost percentage of completion method of recognizing profits. Tony assumed leadership of the business after the recent death of his father, Ton. In reviewing the records, Tony finds the following information regarding a recently completed building project for which the total contract price was P50 million.
Construction in progress account balance 2020 P 10,000,000
How much cost was incurred in 2021?

Romeo Construction has consistently used the percentage of completion method. On January 10, 2020, Romeo began work on the P1,500,000 construction contract. At the inception date, the estimated cost of construction was P1,125,000. The following data relate to the progress of the contract:
DMCI has incurred the following contract costs in the first year on a two-year fixed-price contract for P4 million to construct a bridge:
Other contract cost (including site labor costs)
How much profit or loss should DMCI recognize in the first year of the three-year construction contract?
Loss of P500,000 prorated over two years
Loss of P1 million expensed immediately
No profit or loss in the first year and deferring it to the second year.
Since 60% is the percentage of completion, recognize 60% of the loss.

In 2020, Mega world began construction work under a 3-year contract. The contract price was P800,000. Mega world uses the percentage of completion method for financial accounting purposes. The income to be recognized each year is based on the proportion of costs incurred to total estimated costs for completing the contract. The financial statement presentation relating to this contract on December 31, 2020, were as follows:
Cost of uncompleted contract over billings
What was the initial estimated gross before tax on this contract?

DMCI contractors had a 3-year construction contract in 2020 for P900,000. The company uses the percentage of completion method for financial statement purposes. Income to be recognized each year is based on the ratio of the cost incurred to the total estimated cost to complete the contract. Data on this contract follows:
Net income recognized in 2020 before tax
DMCI contractors maintain a separate bank account for each construction contract. Bank deposits to this contract amounted to P50,000.
What was the estimated total income before the tax on this contract?
CC Corp. owns a subsidiary in Japan whose balance sheet in Japanese Yen for the last years follows:
Relevant exchange rates are (Yen to Peso):

CC formed the subsidiary on January 1, 2020. The income of the subsidiary was earned evenly throughout the years, and the subsidiary declared dividends worth ¥15,000 on September 12, 2020, and none were declared during 2021. How much is the cumulative translation adjustment for 2021?
The foreign exchange gain (loss) on option contract to be recognized in earnings on June 30
The foreign exchange gain (loss) on option contract to be recognized in equity on June 30
thin US territory wherein the functional currency is the US $. The presentation currency of the bank is Philippine peso. The following financial position data for the year 2020 are provided:
The following additional data are provided.
What is the translation gain (loss) to be recognized by BPI US in its Statement of Comprehensive Income for the year ended December 31, 2020?
On October 3, 2020, Mike Inc. entered into a forward contract with BPI Bank for the speculation to buy $100 to be delivered on January 30, 2021. The following direct exchange rates were provided:
What is the net foreign currency gain (loss) to be recognized by Mike for the years ended December 31, 2020, and December 31, 2021, respectively?
Hamon reported sales of 1,500,000 baht during 2014. What amount (rounded) would have been included for this subsidiary in calculating consolidated sales?
On December 31. Hamon had accounts receivable of 280,000 baht. What amount (rounded) would have been included for this subsidiary in calculating consolidated accounts receivable?

Paul Corporation, a Philippine Company, expects to order goods from a foreign supplier at 250,000 foreign currencies, with delivery and payment to be made on October 24. On July 24, Paul purchased a three-month call option for 250,000 foreign currencies and designated this option as a cash flow hedge of a forecasted foreign currency transaction. The following exchange rates apply.
What amount will Paul include as an option expense in net income during the period July 24 to October 24?
For what amount should Brisco's accounts payable be credited on May 8?
How much foreign currency will it cost Brisco to finally pay the payable on June 7?

Rikka Co. ordered parts costing 100,000 baht for a foreign supplier or May 12 when the exchange rate was P.24 per baht. A one-month forward contract was signed on the date to purchase 100,000 baht at a forward rate of P.25 per baht. On June 12, when the parts were received, and payment was made, the exchange rate was P.28 per baht. At what amount should inventory be reported?
Patricia Company, a Philippine Corporation, sold inventory on December 1, 2020, with payment of 10,000 foreign currencies to be received in sixty days. The pertinent exchange rates were as follows:
For what amount should Sales be credited on December 1?

On December 1, 2020, Gary Inc. entered into a 120-day forward contract to purchase 250,000 US dollars for speculative purposes. Gary, Inc., fiscal year ends on December 31. The exchange rates are as follows:
How much is the forex gain or loss to be reported from this forward contract in 2021?
Purchased merchandise from a foreign supplier on January 20, 2020 for the Philippine peso equivalent of P60,000 and paid the invoice on April 20, 2020, at the Philippine peso equivalent of P68,000.

On September 1, 2020, borrowed the Philippine peso equivalent of P300,000 evidence by a note that is payable in the lender's local currency on September 1, 2021. On December 31, 2020, the Philippine peso equivalent of the principal amount was P320,000.
In Jane's income statement, what amount should be included as a foreign exchange loss?
The subsidiary’s functional currency is the local currency unit. What amount should Rose's balance sheet include for the preceding items?
The subsidiary's functional currency is peso. What amount should Rose’s balance sheet include for the preceding items?

Noting that there was a P5,780,000 translation adjustment gain, Hesensi Inc. (parent company) secured a foreign bank loan denominated in the functional currency when the spot rate was 1 Taiwan NT dollar = P1.022. The bank loan has a principal amount of 200,000,000 NT dollars. Interest calculations are ignored. The bank loan is designated as a hedge of net investment and is considered to have satisfied all necessary criteria.
Selected exchange rates between the functional currency (NT dollar) and the peso are as follows:
The December 31, 2020 loans payable account balance obtained to hedge the net investment amounted to

On March 31, 2020, C received from X P550,000 representing franchise fee . Franchise services were immediately started by C, and these were completed on Oct. 31, 2020, at the cost of P330,000. Further, the franchisor is entitled to a 5% fee on gross sales payable within the first ten days of the following month. The following year, the franchisee yielded gross sales of P9,000,000. C's earned franchise fee for the year 2021:

On January 2, 2020: Magnolia Ice Cream signed an agreement authorizing Trisha to operate as a franchisee for an initial franchise fee P500,000 received upon signing of the agreement. Trisha commenced operations on August 1, 2020, at which date all of the initial services required of Magnolia Ice Cream had been performed at the cost of P 120,000. The franchise agreement further provides that Trisha must pay a 10% monthly continuing franchising fee. Sales reported from August 1 to December 31, 2020, amount to P400,000.
What is the net income related to the franchise fee to be reported by Magnolia Ice Cream in 2020?

Ana's Inc. granted a franchise to Mocca for the Makati area. The franchisee was to pay a franchisee of P500,000, payable in five equal annual installments starting with the payment upon signing of the agreement. The franchise was to pay monthly 3% of gross sales of the preceding month. Should the operations of the outlet prove to be unprofitable, the franchise may be canceled with whatever obligations owing Ana's, Inc. in connection with the P500,000 franchise fee waived. The prevailing interest rate is 14%. The first year generated gross sales of P2,500,000.
What is the amount of unearned franchise fee after the first year of operations?

On January 2, 2020, Mycor's Inc. signed an agreement to operate as a franchisee of Mang Inasal for an initial franchise fee of P2,343,750 for ten years. Of this amount, P468,750 was paid when the agreement was signed and the balance payable in three annual payments beginning on Dec ember 31, 2020. Mycor's signed a non-interest bearing note for the balance. The implicit interest rate is 18%. Assume that substantial services amounting to P730,000 had already been rendered by the franchisor, and indirect costs of P53,750 have also been incurred.
If the collection of the note is not reasonably assured, calculate the net income. For the year ended December 31, 2020. Use PV factor 2.17.

On .January 2, 2020, Diversified Enterprises signed a franchise agreement with DTSI Company for an initial franchise fee of P92,500. Of this amount, DTSI paid P17,500 upon the signing of the franchise contract, and the balance is payable in four annual payments of P18,750 starting December 31, 2020. DTS I issued 12% interest-bearing notes for the balance. The collection of notes is not reasonably assured.

The down payment is not refundable; it represents the actual cost of initial services, provided by Diversified before formal signing. However, substantial additional services have yet to be performed by Diversified. During 2020 additional direct franchise costs of P45,750 and indirect cost of P15,000 were incurred by Diversified.

It is also agreed that DTSI will pay a continuing franchise fee at 3% of its gross sales revenue. The franchise outlet commenced business operations on October 1, 2020, and its gross sales totaled, P600,000 by year-end.
The net income recognized by Diversified from the DTSI franchise in 2020 is

Kenneth Company, Inc. franchisor, entered into a franchise agreement with Orville Trading, franchisee, on March 31, 2020. The total franchise fee is P500,000, of which P 100,000 is payable upon signing and the balance in four equal annual installments. The down payment is refundable in the event the franchisor fails to render services, and none thus far had been rendered. When Kenneth Company prepared its financial statements on March 31, 2020, the franchise fee revenue to be reported is:

Jodee Construction's Enterprises, a franchisor, charges franchisees a “franchise fee” of P500,000. Of this amount a non-refundable P200,000 is paid upon the signing of the contract with the balance payable in three equal installments after each year after that. Jodee Construction will assist in locating a suitable business site, conduct a market study, oversee the construction of facilities, and provides initial training for employees.

On December 1, 2020, Jodee Construction' signed a franchising agreement for the U-belt area. By the end of 2020, it was determined that the substantial performance of the initial services had cost Jodee Construction' a total P150,000, and that collection of the balance of the franchise fee has been reasonably assured. In its 2020 income statement.
Jodee Construction’ should report franchise revenue and net income:
The Carl Company uses the installment method of reporting for accounting purposes. The roll owing data were obtained for the years 2020 to 2022:

In 2022, one of the customers defaulted in his payment, and the company repossessed the merchandise with an estimated market value of P30,000. The sales were in 2020, and the unpaid balance on the date of the repossession was P45,000.
Compute the total realized gross profit for 2021.
The Devant Appliances account for its sales on the installment basis. As the beginning of 2022 ledger accounts include the following balances:
At the end of 2022, account balances before adjustment for realized gross profit on installment sales are:

Installment sales in 2022 are made at 25% above the cost of merchandise sold. During 2022 upon default in payment by the customer, the company repossessed the merchandise with an estimated market value of P4,000. The sales were in 2021 for P21,600, and P 12,800 had been collected before repossession.
The realized gross profit on December 31, 2022 is

Cesar Company holds 90% of the common stock of Diane Company During 2020, Cesar reported sales of P1,120,000 and cost of goods sold of P 840,000. For this same period, Diane has sales of P 420,000 and cost of goods sold of P 252,000. Also, during 2020, Cesar sold merchandise to Diane for P 140,000. The subsidiary still possesses 40% of this inventory at the end of 2020.
The new profit and loss ratio of Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, and Joshua, respectively for 2020 is
The share of partners Michael in the 2020 corrected net income is:

The Home Office in Makati shipped merchandise costing P80,000 to the Manila branch and paid for the freight charges of P600. The home office bills the branch at 125% of the cost. Manila branch was subsequently instructed to transfer one-half of the merchandise to Quezon City branch, wherein Quezon City branch paid for P200 freight. If the shipment were made directly from Makati to Quezon City, the freight cost would have been P400. By how much will Manila branch charge the Home Office account?
The following transactions were incurred for the year by the Company:
Transfer of P13,000 merchandise to an agency to establish a working fund.
Receipt of sales orders from the agency, P130,000.
Collection of agency accounts by the home office, P91,000
Home office disbursements are representing agency expenses, P11,700.
Replenishment of the agency working fund upon receipt of expense vouchers for P5,850.
The cost of goods sold identified with agency sales, P93,600.
How much is the net income traceable to the agency?
On December 31, 2017, the home office of Trisha Supply Company recorded a shipment of merchandise to its Glenda branch as follows:
Unrealized profit in Glenda branch inventory

The Glenda branch sells 40% of the merchandise to outside entities during the rest of December 31, 2020. The books of the home office and Trisha branches are closed on December 30 of each year. On January 5, 2021, the Glenda branch transferred half of the original shipments to the Sandy branch, and the Glenda branch pays P500 freight on the shipment.
At what amounts should the 60% of the merchandise remaining unsold on December 31, 2020, be included in the inventory of the Glenda branch on December 31, 2020?

The Rommel Company was organized in 2015. Shortly after opening its doors to the public at the main store, Rommel Company established a branch in another city. At the end of the second year of operations, the home office received the following condensed income statement from the branch:
The management at the home office questioned the accuracy of these figures and assigned you the task of verifying the branch data. Your review of the records uncovered the following facts:
The beginning of year balance in unrealized profit to the branch was P12,000.

During the period, the home office shipped goods to the branch that had cost the main store P150,000. However, your review of the branch receiving reports revealed that several shipments from the home office had been recorded twice by the branch accountant.
The branch is billed a uniform 25% above cost and receives inventory only from the home office.
The branch ending inventory was correctly reported at a billed price of P43,500.

When reconciling reciprocal accounts, you found that the branch had not recorded P4,000 of services performed by the Home Office and billed to the branch. All other selling and administrative expenses were correctly reported by the branch.
Compute the correct net income of the branch.
On July 1, the Joshua Company organized a sales outlet in Cebu City. Following are the home office-branch transactions for July:
July 1 The home office transferred P250,000 to its Cebu branch.
Merchandise costing the home P30 per unit was shipped to the branch at an
invoice price of P40 per unit. Ten thousand units were shipped on July 2; a second
order was to be filled by local suppliers.
Shipping costs on the above were paid as follows:
Additional merchandise was acquired by the branch from regional distributors,
Display equipment was purchased by the home office, cost P360,000, and was
delivered to the branch. Plant assets accounts were kept by the home office.
Branch sales for the period July 3 - 10; on account, 8,000 units at PS0.
Branch sales for the period July 11-24; on account, 5,000 units at PS0
Cash remittance by a branch to the home office, P 100,000
Monthly summary of branch cash expenses: Advertising (P4,000); Sales
Depreciation recorded by the home office for July included PI 5,000 that related
to the display equipment used by the branch. Insurance on this equipment was
amortized by the home office in the amount of P2,500.
Inventories of merchandise at the branch on July 31 included the following:
From the home office (1,500 units x P40)
From local suppliers (500 units x P31)
Determine the correct balance of the reciprocal account after recording branch net income or loss.
The pre-closing general ledger trial balances at December 31, 2020, for the Ropali Company and its Naga City branch office are shown below:
Your audit disclosed the following data:

On December 23, the branch office manager purchased P400,000 of furniture and fixtures failed to notify the home office. The bookkeeper knowing that all fixed assets are carried on the home office, recorded the proper entry on the branch office records. It is the Company’s policy not to take any depreciation assets acquired in the last half of a year.

On December 27, a branch office customer erroneously paid his account of P200,000 to the home office. The bookkeeper made the correct entry on the home office books but did not notify the branch office.
On December 30, the branch office remitted cash of P500,000, which was received the home office in January 2021
On December 3, the branch office erroneously recorded the December allocated expenses from the home office as P50,000 instead of P150,000.
On December 31, the home office shipped merchandise billed at P300,000 to the branch office, which was received in January 2020.

The entire opening inventory of the branch office had been purchased from the home office, Home office 2020 shipments to the branch offices were purchased by the home office in 2020. The physical inventories at December 31, 2020, excluding the shipment in transit, are:
Branch office 2,000,000 (comprised of P 1,800,000 from home office and
7. The home office consistently bills shipments to the branch office at 20% above cost. The sales account is credited for the invoice price.
How much is the correct net income of the branch?

Aliza Trading established a branch in Quezon City to distribute part of the goods purchased by it from other suppliers. Aliza ships merchandise to the branch at 20% above cost. The following account balances are taken from ledger balances of the home office and the branch:

The income statement submitted by the Balanga Branch to the Home Office for December 2020 is shown below. After effecting the necessary adjustments, the true net income of the branch was ascertained to be P 156,000.
The billing price based on cost imposed by the home office to the branch and

The Angelo Corporation decided to open a branch store in Balayan. Shipments of merchandise to the branch totaled P108,000, which included a 20% mark-up on cost. All accounting records are to be kept at the home office. The branch submitted the following report summarizing its operations for the period ended on December 31, 2020.
Inventory on hand, December 31 (80% from home office)
The branch operations resulted in a net income (loss) of:
106. Following data were taken from the books of Sony Corporation from 2020:
The unpaid balance of prior year's installment of contracts
The value assigned to repossessed merchandise
The unrealized gross profit on the 20 I 5 installment sales as of December 31, 2016, was

Hsensi Company sells goods for cash, on normal credit (2/10, n/30). However, on July 1, 2020, the Company sold a used computer for P22,000; the inventory carrying value was P4,400. The company collected P2,000 cash and agreed to let the customer make payments on the P20,000 whenever possible during the next 12 months. The company management stated that it had no reliable basis for estimating the probability of default. The following additional data are available: (a) collection on the installment receivable during 2020 were 3,000, and during 2021 were P2,000, and (b) on December 1, 2021, Hsensi repossessed the computer (estimated net realizable value, P7,000).
Determine the realized gross profit on installment sales for the year 2020.

108. Al Corporation has been using the cash method to account for income since its first year of operations in 2020. All sales are made on credit with notes receivable given by the customers. The income statements for 2020 and 2021 included the following amounts:
Cost of goods purchased which includes an increase in
inventory of goods on hand of P4,000 in 2015 and P16,000
The balances due on the notes at the end of each year were as follows:
Under the installment method, how much is the realized gross profit in 2021?
Presented below is the unadjusted trial balance of Rainbow Products Corporation at December 31, 2020:
The cost of goods sold had been uniform, over the years at 60% of sales.
Rainbow Products Corporation adopts perpetual inventory procedures. On installment sales, the corporation charges installment accounts receivable and credits inventory gross profit accounts.
Repossessions of merchandise have been made during 2020 due to some customer's failure to pay maturing installments. Analyses of these transactions were summarized as follows:

The repossessed merchandise was unsold on December 31, 2020. It was ascertained that they were booked upon repossession at original costs. A fair valuation of these items would be a sale price of the repossessed merchandise at P 10,000 after incurring costs of reconditioning of P5,000 and cost to dispose of them in the market at P500.
What is the realized gross profit of 2020 sales was:
Joel and Jonats formed a joint venture on January 1, 2013, to operate two stores to be managed by each venturer/participants. They agreed to contribute cash as follows:

Profits and losses are to be divided in the capital ratio. All venture transactions are for cash. Cash receipt and disbursements of the business during the four months handled through the participant's venturers bank accounts are as follows:
On April 30, the remaining non-cash venture assets in the hands of the participants/venturers were sold for P60,000. The venture is terminated and settled is made between Joel and Jonats.
The P60,000 is divided between the participants/venturers as follows:

Ross and Reggie formed a joint venture to purchase and sell a special type of merchandise. The venturers agreed to contribute cash of P270,000 each to be used in purchasing the merchandise and to share profits and losses equally. They also agreed that each should record the purchases, sales, and expenses in their books.
Upon termination of the joint venture, the following data are made available:
Expenses paid from joint venture cash
A hospital has the following account balances:
What is the hospital's net patient service revenue?
113. USC, a nonprofit university, received the following cash contributions from donors during the year 2020:
Contributions restricted by donors for scholarship programs
Contributions from a donor who stipulated that the money be spent in
accordance with the wishes of the hospital's board of trustees
Contributions restricted by donors for equipment acquisitions

Assuming the university spent P 75,000-of the donors' contributions for scholarship programs on financing this year's scholars, how much should be included in its current fund's revenue for the year ended December 31, 2020?
114. Wood Den. a private, not-for-profit zoological society, received contributions restricted for
research totaling P40,000 in 2017. None of the contributions were spent on research in 2017. In 2020, P28,000 of the contributions were used to support the research activities of the society.
The net effect on the statement of activities for the year ended December 31, 2020, for Wood Den
P 12,000 increase in temporarily restricted net assets.
P28,000 decrease in temporarily restricted net assets.
P28,000 increase in unrestricted net assets.
P28,000 decrease in unrestricted net assets.
115. St. Luke's Hospital, a nonprofit hospital-affiliated, received the following cash contributions from donors during the year ended December 31, 2020:
Contributions restricted by donors for research
Contributions restricted by donors for capital acquisitions

Neither of the contributions was spent during 2020; however, during 2021, the hospital spent the entire P50,000 contribution on research and the entire P250,000 contribution on a capital asset, which was placed into service during the year. On the hospital's statement of Operations for the year ended December 31, 2021, what total amount should be reported for “net assets released from restrictions.”

TD decided to withdraw from his partnership with SM and MR. Before his withdrawal, TD's capital balance was P 58,000, while SM's was P64,000, and MR's was P77,000. Also, the partnership's total assets amounted to P 450,000, but the partners agreed that a fixed asset was under depreciated by P 15,000. TD, SM, and MR share profits and losses in the ratio of 2:4:4, respectively, If TD was paid P53,200 upon his retirement, how much is the remaining partnership net assets after TD's withdrawal?

Hannah and Blanca, having capital balances of P 140,000 P 75,000 respectively, decided to admit Jet in to their partnership. Jet is to invest a sufficient amount to have a 25% interest in the partnership. If Hannah and Blanca share profit in a proportion of 3:1, respectively, and Blanca's capital balance after Jet's investment is P84,250, how much was invested by Jet?

Dorian and Donnie are partners who share profits and losses in a 2:3 ratio. The partners liquidated in installments. Some noncash assets have been sold, but other assets with a book value of P126,000 remain. Liabilities are now P16,000, and liquidation expenses are expected to be P7,200. The capital balances are P92,000 for Dorian and P68,000 for Donnie.
Assuming the available cash is distributed, how much is the share of Dorian?
On January 1, 2020, A, B, and C fanned a partnership with capital contributions as follows:

The partnership agreement stipulates that each partner shall receive a 5% interest in capital contributed and that A and a shall receive salaries of P5,000 and P3 ,000, respectively. The agreement further provides that C shall receive a minimum of P2,500 per annum and D a minimum of P6,000 which is inclusive of amounts representing interest and their respective shares in partnership profits. The balance of the profits shall be distributed among A, B, C, and D in the ratio of 3:3:2:2.

What amount must be earned by the partnership in 2020, before any charges for interest and partners' salaries so that A may receive an aggregate of P 12,500 including interest, salary, and share of profits?

The partners of the M&N Partnership started liquidating their business on July 1, 2020, at which time the partners were sharing profits and losses 40% to M and 60% to N. The balance sheet of the partnership appeared as follows:
During July, the partners collected P600 of the receivables with no loss. The partners also sold during the month the entire inventory on which they realized a total of P32,400.
How much of the cash was paid to M's capital on July 31, 2020?
A partnership begins its first year with the following capital balances:
The articles of partnership stipulate that profits and losses be assigned in the following manner:
Each partner is allocated interest equal to 10% of the beginning capital balance.
Dad a is allocated compensation of P20,000 per year.
Any remaining profits and losses are allocated on a 3:3:4 basis, respectively.
Each partner is allowed to withdraw up to P5,000 cash per year.
Assuming that the net income is PS0,000 and that each partner withdraws the maximum amount allowed, what is the balance in Debbie's capital account at the end of that year?
122. The following are the information regarding partnership business:

Profits and losses are split as follows: Barn: (20%), Bunny (30%), and Costello (50%). Costello wants to leave the partnership and is paid P100,000 from the business based on provisions in the articles of partnership. If the partnership uses the bonus method, what is the balance of Burn's capital account after Costello withdraws?
At year-end, the Eine Partnership has the following capital balances:

Profits and losses are split on a 3:3:2:2 basis, respectively. Craig decides to leave the partnership and is paid P90,000 from the business based on the original contractual agreement. If the goodwill method is to be applied, what is the balance of Montana's capital account after Craig withdraws?
A local partnership is liquidating and is currently reporting the following capital balances:
Angela, capital (50% share of all profits and losses)

Cassidy has indicated that a forthcoming contribution will cover the P12,000 deficit. However, the two remaining partners have asked to receive the P25,000 in cash that is presently available. How much of this money should each of the partners be given?
A partnership has the following balance sheet just before the final liquidation is to begin:
Liquidation expenses are estimated to be P 12,000. The other assets are sold for P40,000. What distribution can be made to the partners?
126. What is the total agreed capitalization of the ABC Partnership?
127. What is the cash to be contributed by C in the A BC Partnership?
What is the net income or net loss of ABC Partnership for the year ended December 31, 2020?
130. What is the capital balance of Con December 31, 2020?
130. What is the partnership profit for the year ended December 3 1, 2020?
What is A's share in partnership profit for 2020?
133. What is B's share in partnership profit for 2020?
133. On December 31, 2020, the Statement of Financial Position of ABC Partnership provided the following data with profit or loss ratio of 1:6:3:
On January 1, 2021, D is admitted to the partnership by purchasing 40% of the capital interest of B at a price of P500,000.
What is the capital balance of B after the admission of D on January 1, 2021?

134. On December 31, 2020, ABC Partnership's Statement of Financial Positions showed that A, B, and C have capital balances of P500,000, P300,000, and P200,000 with profit or loss ratio of 1:3:6. On January 1, 2021, C retired from the partnership and received P350 000. At the time of C's retirement, an asset of the partnership is undervalued.
What is the capital balance of A after the retirement of C?

On December 31, 2020, ABC Partnership's Statement of Financial Position showed that A, B, and C have capital balances of P400,000, P300,000, and P 100,000 with profit or loss ratio of 1:4:5. On January 1, 2021, C retired from the partnership and received P80,000. At the time of C's retirement, the assets and liabilities of the partnership are properly valued.
What is the capital balance of B after the retirement of C?
On December 31, 2020, the Statement of Financial Position of ABC Partnership provided the following data with profit or loss ratio of 1:6:3:
On January 1, 2021, D is admitted to the partnership by investing P 1,000,000 to the partnership for 20% capital interest.
If all the assets of the existing partnership are- properly valued what is the capital balance of C after the admission of D?
What is the capital balance of D after his admission to the partnership?
What is the capital balance of C after the admission of D to the Partnership?
How much cash was received by Bat the end of partnership liquidation?
140. How much cash was received by C at the end of partnership liquidation?
141. What is the amount of cash received by partner Con January 31, 2019?
142. What is the share of B in the maximum possible loss on January 31, 2021?
What is the amount of total cash withheld on January 31, 2021?
What is the amount received by the holder of the note payable at the end of corporation liquidation?
145. What is the amount received by the holder of the mortgage payable at the end of corporate liquidation?
146. What is the amount received by the employees at the end of corporate liquidation concerning their salaries?
147. What is the amount received by the holder of accounts payable at the end of liquidation?
148. What is the amount of net free assets available at the end of liquidation?
What is the amount of total assets to be reported by Entity A concerning its interest in Entity C?
150. What is the amount of sales revenue to be reported by Entity A concerning its interest in Entity C?
151. What is the amount of total liabilities to be reported by Entity B concerning its interest in Entity C?
152. What is the balance of Investment in Entity C to be reported by Entity A in its Statement of Financial Position on December 31, 2019?
153. What is the balance of Investment in Entity C to be reported by Entity B in its Statement of financial Position on December 31, 2021?
154. What is the investment income to be reported by Entity A for the year ended December 31, 2020?
155. What is the balance of Investment in Entity C to be reported by Entity B on December 31, 2020?
156. If Entity A elected fair value model to account for its investment in entity C, what is the net effect on Entity A's profit or loss for the year ended December 31, 2020?
157. If Entity B elected equity method to account for its investment in Entity C, what is the carrying
amount of Entity B's Investment in Entity C on December 31, 2020?
158. If Entity an elected cost method to account for its Investment in Entity C, what is the carrying amount of Entity A's Investment in Entity C on December 31, 2020?
If Entity B elected equity method to account for its Investment in Entity C, what is the net effect in Entity B's profit or loss for the year ended December 31, 2020?
160. What is the net income reported by the branch in its separate income statement for the current year?
161. What is the ending inventory to be reported by the entity in its combined statement of financial
163. What is the overstatement in the cost of goods sold reported by the branch in its separate income statement for the current year?

163. The home office in Quezon City ships and bills merchandise to its provincial branch at cost. The branch carries its accounts receivable and makes its collections. The branch also pays its expenses. The branch transactions for 2020 are reflected in the following information:
What is the balance of the Investment in the Branch account in the home office book?
164. What is the branch inventory on December 31, 2020, at cost?
165. What is the net branch income for the current year?
166. What is the goodwill or gain on bargain purchase arising from business combination?
167. What total amount should be expensed as incurred at the time of business combination?
168. What is Entity A’s amount of total assets after the business combination?

169. Entity A acquired the net assets of Entity B by issuing 10,000 ordinary shares with a par value of P10 and bonds payable with a face amount of P500,000. The bonds are classified as a financial liability at amortized cost.

At the time of the acquisition, the ordinary shares are publicly quoted at P20 per share. On the other hand, the bonds payable are trading at 110. Entity A paid P10,000 share issuance costs and P20,000 bond issue costs. Entity A also paid P40,000 acquisition-related costs and P30,000 indirect costs of the business combination.
Before the date of acquisition, Entity A and Entity B reported the following data:

At the time of the acquisition, the current assets of Entity A have fair valve of P 1,200,000, while the noncurrent assets of Entity B have fair value of P 1,300,000. On the same date, the current liabilities of Entity B have fair value of P600,000, while the noncurrent liabilities of Entity A have a fair value of P500,000.
What is Entity A's amount of total liabilities after the business combination?
170. What is the initial measurement of noncontrolling interest in net assets in the consolidated
171. What is the goodwill or gain on bargain purchase arising from business combination?
172. What is the gain on remeasurement of the existing investment in Entity B as a result of step
174. What is the goodwill or gain on bargain purchase as a result of the business combination?
174. What is the noncontrolling interest in net assets on December 31, 2020?
175. What is the consolidated net income attributable to parent shareholders for the year ended
176. What is the amount of consolidated retained earnings on December 31, 2020?
177. What is the consolidated sales revenue for the year ended on December 31, 2021?
178. What is the consolidated gross profit for the year ended December 31, 2021?
179. What is the noncontrolling interest in net income for the year ended December 31, 2020?
180. What is the consolidated net income attributable to parent's shareholders for the year ~ndee:J
What is the consolidated depreciation expense of machinery for 2021?
182. What is the consolidated carrying amount of machinery on December 31, 2021?
183. What is the noncontrolling interest in net income for 2020?
185. What is the consolidated net income attributable to parent shareholders for 2021?
185. On January 1, 2021, Entity A acquired 90% of outstanding ordinary shares of Entity B at a price of P900,000. Entity A paid P20,000 costs related to the acquisition of shares.

At the acquisition date, the net assets of Entity B were reported at P950,000. All the assets of Entity B are properly valued except for machinery, which is undervalued by P 150.000. The machinery has a remaining useful life of 5 years.
For the year ended December 3 1, 2021, Entity B reported a net income of P200,000 and declared dividends in the amount of P30,000.
The fair value of Investment in Entity B on December 31, 2021, is P1,000,000, while the cost of disposal is 5%.
Entity A voluntarily prepared its separate financial statements.
If Entity A elects cost method to account its Investment in Entity a in its separate financial statements, what is the carrying amount of the Investment in Entity B on December 31 2021?
186. What is the investment income for 2021 if Entity A elects cost method to account its Investment in Entity B in its separate financial statements?
187. What is the net effect in profit or loss for 2021 if Entity A elects fair value model to account its Investment in Entity B in its separate financial statements?
188. If Entity A elects fair value model to account its Investment in Entity B in its separate financial Statements, what is the carrying amount of the Investment in Entity Bon December 31, 2021?
189. What is the amount of permanently restricted net assets at the end of the first year?
190. What is the amount of temporarily restricted net assets at the end of the year?
191. What is the amount of unrestricted net assets at the end of the year?
192. In the statement of activities of the NPO for the year ended December 31, 2020, which of the following is the proper effect of the transactions
a. Increase in temporarily restricted net assets by P100,000
b. Increase in unrestricted net assets by P1,000,000
c. Increase in unrestricted net assets by P16,000
d. Decrease in temporarily restricted net assets by P20,000
In the statement of activities of the NPO for the year ended December 31, 2021, which of the following is the proper effect of the transactions?
a. Increase in temporarily restricted net assets by P100,000.
b. Increase in unrestricted net assets by P1,000,000.
c. Increase in unrestricted net assets by P 16,000.
d. Decrease in temporarily restiricted assets by P 100,000
194. How should the cash flows be reported in NPO's Statement of Cash Flows for the year ended
a. Cash receipts from operating activities by P100,000.
b. Cash receipts from financing activities by P1,100,000.
c. Cash disbursements for investing activities by P20 000.
d. Cash disbursements for investing activities by P100,000.
195. How should the cash flows be reported in NPO's Statement of Cash Flows for the year ended
Cash receipts from operating activities by PI 00,000.
Cash receipts from financing activities by Pl, 100,000.
Cash disbursements for investing activities by PS0,000.
Cash disbursements for financing activities by P 1,000,000

197. On September 1, 2020, Bain Company received an order for equipment from a foreign customer for 300,000 local cu1Tency units (LCU) when the US dollar equivalent was P96,000. Bain shipped the equipment on October 15, 2018, and billed the customer for 300,000 LCU when the US dollar equivalent was P 100,000. Bain received the customer remittance in full on November 16, 2018, and sold the 300,000 LCU for P 105,000. In the income statement for the year ended December 31, 2018, what amount should Bain report as part of net income a foreign exchange transaction gain?

197. On September 1, 2020, Cano Company, a US corporation, sold merchandise to a foreign firm for 250,000 Botswana pula. Terms of the sale require payment in pula on February 1, 2021. On September 1, 2020, the spot exchange rate was P.20 per pula. On December 31 2020 Cano's yearend, the spot rate was P.19, but the rate increased to P.22 by February 1, 2021, when payment was received. How much should Cano report as foreign exchange transaction gain or loss as part of 2021 income?

198. Hunt Company purchased the merchandise for £300,000 from a vendor in London on November 30, 2020. Payment in British pounds was due on January 30, 2021. The exchange rates to purchase one pound were as follows:
In the income statement, what amount should Hunt report as foreign exchange transaction gain as part of net income?
199. Ball Company had the following foreign currency transactions during 2020:
Merchandise was purchased from a foreign supplier on January 20, 2020, for the US dollar equivalent of P90,000. The invoice was paid on March 20, 2020, at the US dollar equivalent of P96,000.
On July 1, 2020, Ball borrowed the US dollar equivalent of P500,000 evidenced by a note

payable in the lender's local currency on July 1, 2020. On December 31, 2020, the VS dollar equivalents of the principal amount and accrued interest were P520,000 and P26,000, respectively. Interest on the note is 10% per annum.
In Ball's 2020 income statement, what amount should be included as foreign exchange transaction loss as part of net income?

200. On November 1, 2020. Tyrola Publishing Company, located in Colorado, executed a contract with Ernest Blyton, an author from Canada, providing for payment of 10% royalties on Canadian sales of Blyton's book. Payment is to be made in Canadian dollars each January 10 for the previous year's sales. Canadian sales of the book for the year ended December 31, 2019, totaled P50,000 Canadian. Tyrola paid Blyton his 2021 royalties on January 10, 2022. Tyrola's 2021 financial statements were issued on February 1, 2022. Spot rates for Canadian dollars were as follows:
How much should Tyrola accrue for royalties payable on December 31, 2021?
On November 1, 2022, an entity acquired on account goods from a foreign supplier at the cost of P 1,000. The accounts payable are paid on January 30, 2023.
On December 1, 2022, an entity sold on account the said goods to a foreign customer at a selling price of P 1,500. The accounts receivable are collected on February 28, 2023.
The entity is operating in the Philippine economy wherein the functional currency is the Philippine Peso.
The following direct exchange rates are provided:
201. What is the sales revenue for 2022?
202. What is the carrying amount of accounts receivable on December 31, 2022?
203. What is the carrying amount of accounts payable on December 31, 2020?
204. What is the net foreign currency gain for 2022?
Entity A owns majority of the outstanding ordinary shares of Entity B which is operating in the
United States of America wherein the functional currency is the USA P. However, the presentation
currency of Entity B is the Philippine Peso because that is the presentation currency of Entity A.
For the year ended December 31, 2022, Entity B presented its Statement of Financial Position in its
1. The ordinary shares are issued on January 1, 2021, while the preference shares are issued on
2. B reported P 1,000 net income during 2022 and declared dividends in the amount of P200 on
3. The translated amount of retained earnings on December 31, 2021, is P300,000.
The following direct exchange rates are provided:
205. What is the amount of net assets in US dollars on December 31, 2021?
206. What amount of translation gain as a component of other comprehensive income should be
presented in the statement of comprehensive income for the year ended December 31, 2022?
208. What is the translated retained earnings balance on December 31, 2022?
209. What is the cumulative translation credit that should be presented in the statement of financial
209. What is the foreign currency gain or loss on the hedged item for 2020?
210. What is the foreign currency gain or loss on the hedging instrument for 2023?
211. What amount of foreign currency transaction gain from the forward contract should be included in the net income for 2020?
212. What amount of foreign currency transaction loss should be included in income from the

213. On December 1, 2020, Winston Company entered into a forward contract to purchase P 10,000 in 90 days to hedge a commitment to purchase equipment being manufactured to the entity's specifications. The expected delivery date is March 1, 2021, at which time settlement is due to the manufacturer. The hedge qualifies as a fair value hedge. The relevant exchange rates are:
What amount of foreign currency transaction gain from the forward contract should be included in net income for 2020?

On November 1, 2022, Entity A entered into a firm commitment for the exportation of goods at a price of P2,000 . Delivery will happen on January 31, 2022. To hedge this foreign currency denominated firm commitment, Entity A entered into a forward contract with a bank to sell P2,000. Entity A is operating in Philippine economy where the functional currency is Philippine peso. Entity A elects to use fair value hedge to account this hedge of firm commitment. The following direct exchange rates are given:
214. What is the carrying amount of firm commitment asset or liability on December 31, 2022?
215. What is the foreign currency gain or loss on the hedging instruments for 2023?
216. What is the unrealized holding gain or loss to be recognized as a component of other comprehensive income in the statement of comprehensive income for the year ended December 31, 2022?
217. What is the cumulative unrealized gain or loss before reclassification to be reported as a component of other comprehensive income in the Statement of Changes in equity on December 31, 2023?
218. What is the unrealized holding gain or loss to be recognized as component of other comprehensive income in the statement of comprehensive income for the year ended December 31, 2023?
219. What is the cost of equipment in Philippine peso on January 31, 2021?
On November 1, 2022, Entity A anticipated the purchase of inventory on January 31, 2023, at a
price of P 1,000 . To hedge this highly probable forecasted importation, Entity A acquired a call
option from a bank, giving it the right to purchase P1 ,000 at an option price of P40 by paying an
option premium of P300 . Entity A is operating in Philippine economy where the functional
currency is Philippine peso. The following data are provided:
The fair value of call option
Entity A imported the goods on the date anticipated. Afterward, Entity A was able to resell 30% of
220. What is the unrealized holding gain or to be recognized as a component of other comprehensive income in the statement of comprehensive income for the year ended December 31, 2022?
